items.q: What is the difference between OR and XOR?
items.correct: OR is true when at least one input is true; XOR is true when exactly one input is true.
items.misconception: They are the same gate with different names.
items.random_wrong: OR is for numbers, XOR is for letters.
items.why: Students miss the critical case: when BOTH inputs are 1. OR gives 1, XOR gives 0. XOR is 'exclusive' - excludes the both-true case.

items.q: What is abstraction in computer science?
items.correct: Removing unnecessary detail to focus on essential features of a problem.
items.misconception: Making something abstract art or vague.
items.random_wrong: Writing code that does not work.
items.why: Students borrow the everyday meaning of 'vague'. In CS, abstraction is a precise technique: ignoring details that don't matter for the current task, while keeping what does.

items.q: What does it mean that RAM is volatile?
items.correct: Its contents are lost when power is removed.
items.misconception: It can explode or be damaged easily.
items.why: Students take 'volatile' from chemistry literally. In computing, volatile means contents don't persist without power - that's why RAM is used for working memory, not storage.

items.q: Dijkstra's algorithm fails when edges have...
items.why: Correct answer: Negative weights. “Equal weights” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What does the 'h' in A*'s f(n) = g(n) + h(n) represent?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Heuristic estimate to goal. “History cost” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many queens are placed in the classic N-queens problem when N = 8?
items.why: The real figure is 8 queens. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
